# Startup Files, PATH, and Portable Dotfiles

The shell you type into is not the shell your scripts, your
`ssh host 'cmd'` calls, and your cron jobs run in. They are
often different shells entirely (a cron job usually runs
`/bin/sh`, a script may run bash, your terminal may run zsh),
invoked in different modes, and each reads different startup
files, or none at all. A setting that lives in
a file only your interactive terminal reads is invisible to all
three of the others, which is why the single most common shell
complaint is some version of "it works when I type it, but the
script (or the SSH command, or the cron job) cannot find it." The
fix is almost never a new setting; it is putting the setting
where the situation that needs it will actually read it.

This chapter cashes the promissory notes the earlier chapters
left here: the `cp -i`/`mv -i` aliases of Chapter 5, the
`set -o noclobber` of Chapter 7, the history hygiene of
Chapter 10, and the `zoxide`/`fzf` hooks and guarded aliases of
Chapter 16 all live in a startup file, and this is where they go.
But the deliverable is not the list of lines. It is the
placement rule, because the same line helps or does nothing
depending only on which file you put it in.

A note on where the output comes from. The bash startup order and
the non-interactive environment are captured in this book's Linux
sandbox (GNU bash 5.1.16); the zsh order, the macOS bash 3.2
floor, `launchd`, and a terminal's login state are captured on
the author's Mac (macOS 26.5.1, zsh 5.9, bash 3.2.57); and the
"fails over SSH" seam is captured from the Mac against a real
Linux server, the machine of Chapter 14. Startup-file behavior is
the most folklore-ridden topic in this book, so every ordering
claim here is confirmed by a real invocation and only pinned to
the bash and zsh manuals for the reasons behind it
(`verification/chapter-18.md`, all accessed 2026-07-07). The
local order demos were run against a throwaway `HOME` seeded with
demo rc files, so no real local dotfile was read; the one
exception is the remote SSH capture, which forces a login shell
(`bash -lc`) and so does execute the server's real login profile,
but it runs only `echo`/`command -v`, writes nothing, and shows
only the resulting `PATH`. Paths and the account name are masked
(`/home/[account]`, machine-specific mounts to `/mnt/[mount]`),
and long `PATH` tails are
elided with a marked `[...]`.

## Which file loads when

Two yes/no questions decide which startup files a shell reads. Is
it a *login* shell (the first shell of a session, the one a fresh
terminal tab usually gives you)? And is it *interactive* (reading
commands you type, as opposed to running a script)? Bash answers
those two questions with three different files [-@gnubash]. A login shell
reads `/etc/profile` and then the first of `~/.bash_profile`,
`~/.bash_login`, `~/.profile` that exists. An interactive shell
that is not a login shell reads `~/.bashrc`. A non-interactive
shell, one running a script, reads neither. The demo below puts a
marker in each file and then asks bash to run `true` (a
do-nothing command) in each mode:

```bash
$ tail -n +1 .bash_profile .bashrc .profile
==> .bash_profile <==
echo 'reading ~/.bash_profile'

==> .bashrc <==
echo 'reading ~/.bashrc'

==> .profile <==
echo 'reading ~/.profile'

$ bash -lc 'true'          # login (a macOS Terminal tab)
reading ~/.bash_profile

$ bash -i -c 'true' 2>/dev/null   # non-login interactive
reading ~/.bashrc

$ bash -c 'true'           # non-interactive: script, ssh 'cmd'
```

The third command printed nothing: a non-interactive bash read no
startup file at all. (The one exception is the `BASH_ENV`
variable: if it names a file, a script sources it first. Almost
no one sets it, and you should not rely on it being set.) One
more detail worth knowing is the fallback: a login shell reads
the first of the three profile files that exists, so on a machine
with only `~/.profile` and no `~/.bash_profile`, the login shell
reads `~/.profile`.

Zsh answers the same two questions with a different, and in one
way more forgiving, set of files:

```bash
$ zsh -l -i -c 'true' 2>/dev/null   # login + interactive
reading ~/.zshenv
reading ~/.zprofile
reading ~/.zshrc
reading ~/.zlogin

$ zsh -i -c 'true' 2>/dev/null      # non-login interactive
reading ~/.zshenv
reading ~/.zshrc

$ zsh -c 'true'                      # non-interactive script
reading ~/.zshenv
```

The headline is the last block: `~/.zshenv` runs even for a
non-interactive `zsh -c` script, where bash read nothing. That
makes `~/.zshenv` the natural home for anything every zsh must
see, and the zsh manual specifically warns to keep it small for
exactly that reason [-@zshmanual]. The rest follows the same login/interactive
logic as bash: a login shell adds `~/.zprofile` and, last,
`~/.zlogin`; an interactive shell adds `~/.zshrc`. Put the two
shells side by side and the whole map is one table:

| Invoked as | bash reads | zsh reads |
|---|---|---|
| login + interactive | `/etc/profile`, then the first of `~/.bash_profile`, `~/.bash_login`, `~/.profile` | `.zshenv`, `.zprofile`, `.zshrc`, `.zlogin` |
| non-login interactive | `~/.bashrc` | `.zshenv`, `.zshrc` |
| non-interactive (`bash -c`, `ssh host 'cmd'`, a script) | nothing, unless `$BASH_ENV` names a file | `.zshenv` only |

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
Whether a new terminal tab is a *login* shell is decided by the
terminal emulator, not the operating system, and this is where
the folklore "macOS opens a login shell" is only half a truth.
On the author's Mac, both Terminal.app and WezTerm open a
login + interactive shell (`[[ -o login ]]` reports `login shell`
in a real tab), so a Mac tab reads `.zprofile` *and* `.zshrc`.
Many Linux terminal emulators instead open a non-login
interactive shell, so a Linux tab reads only `.bashrc` (or
`.zshrc`), never the profile. That is why the same
`.bash_profile`-versus-`.bashrc` split behaves differently on the
two platforms, and why a PATH edit a Mac user puts in
`.bash_profile` can silently do nothing for a Linux user, and the
reverse. Test your own tab with `[[ -o login ]]` in zsh or
`shopt -q login_shell` in bash; do not use `echo $0`, whose
leading-dash convention is a bash-only heuristic and does not
report login state in zsh.
:::

## PATH, and why order matters

`PATH` is the list of directories the shell searches to turn a
command name into a program (Chapter 2). Two things about it are
a startup-file question: where you set it so it persists, and in
what order its directories sit. Chapter 3 left both here. When
Homebrew's installer finishes, it prints a line to add,
`eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"`, and appends it to
`~/.zprofile`; tools installed by `uv` and many Rust programs
land in `~/.local/bin`, which has to be on PATH the same way. The
question this chapter answers is which file those lines go in,
and the answer follows directly from the table above.

`~/.zprofile` is a login file, which on a Mac covers every
Terminal tab, so the `brew shellenv` line there makes `brew` and
its packages work in your interactive shells. But a login file is
*not* read by a non-interactive `zsh -c` or a script, so if a
scheduled job or an `ssh host 'cmd'` needs `brew`'s PATH, the
line belongs in `~/.zshenv` instead, the file read on every
invocation. The trade is real: `~/.zshenv` runs for every shell,
including the fast ones, so keep it to the few `export` lines
that truly must be universal. A short, honest split is:

```bash
# ~/.zshenv  (read on EVERY zsh invocation: PATH and env only)
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"

# ~/.zprofile  (login shells; Homebrew's installer added it)
eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
```

Order is the second half. The shell searches PATH left to right
and runs the first match, so a directory placed at the front
shadows the same-named program further back. Prepending
`~/.local/bin` is usually what you want: your `uv`-installed tool
wins over an older system copy. But front-of-PATH is also the
hijack risk Chapters 3 and 7 raised: a writable directory early
on PATH lets anything dropped into it, a fake `ls` or `git`,
shadow the real command. So prepend only directories you trust
and control, and if you ever add a project-local `bin`, append it
rather than prepend it. You can see the ordering take effect: a
PATH edit made in an interactive rc puts its directory at the
front of the list the next interactive shell sees.

```bash
$ cat .bashrc
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
echo 'reading ~/.bashrc'

$ bash -i -c 'echo "$PATH"' 2>/dev/null   # rc ran
reading ~/.bashrc
/home/[account]/.local/bin:/usr/local/sbin:[...]
```

## The portable dotfile

Now the promissory notes come due. The aliases, the `noclobber`
setting, the history option, and the modern-kit hooks are all
*interactive* comforts: things you want at a prompt, not in a
script. So they belong in the interactive file, `~/.zshrc` (or
`~/.bashrc`), and nowhere else. Here is a compact, portable
version that delivers each promise in one line:

```bash
# ~/.zshrc  (interactive niceties, read by interactive shells)

# safety at the prompt (Chapters 5 and 7)
alias cp='cp -i'
alias mv='mv -i'
set -o noclobber

# history hygiene (Chapter 10)
setopt HIST_IGNORE_SPACE   # bash: HISTCONTROL=ignorespace

# modern kit, only if it is installed (Chapter 16)
command -v eza    >/dev/null && alias ls='eza'
command -v bat    >/dev/null && alias cat='bat'
command -v zoxide >/dev/null && eval "$(zoxide init zsh)"
command -v fzf    >/dev/null && source <(fzf --zsh)
```

That last line is the `fzf` shell integration Chapter 16
deferred: it installs the `Ctrl-R` history search and `Ctrl-T`
file picker key bindings, and, being guarded, it is skipped on a
box without `fzf`.

Two features of that file matter more than the individual lines.
First, the `command -v <tool> >/dev/null &&` guard in front of
each kit line is the graceful degradation Chapter 16 promised:
the alias or hook is installed only if the tool is actually
present, so the very same `~/.zshrc`, copied to a bare server
from Chapter 14 where `eza` and `zoxide` do not exist, simply
skips those lines. `ls` stays the real `ls`, nothing errors, and
your dotfile is portable instead of a pile of "command not found"
on every login. Second, the placement itself: putting these in an
interactive file is not just tidy, it is a correctness boundary,
because an alias or option set here protects you at the prompt
and *does not* protect a script. A script runs non-interactively,
never reads `~/.zshrc`, and does not expand your aliases anyway;
so `set -o noclobber` in your rc will not stop a script's `>`
from clobbering a file, and `alias rm='rm -i'` will not make a
script's `rm` ask. That is the same interactive-only caveat
Chapters 5 and 7 attached to these settings, and it is why a
script that must be safe sets its own options (Chapter 11) rather
than trusting your prompt's.

It helps to see the files as a set and remember which loads when.
The listing beside the tree is a real throwaway home; the tree is
the map, and the live order demos above are the proof each file
fires when the map says.

```text
$ ls -a
. .. .zlogin .zprofile .zshenv .zshrc
```

```text
~/                home directory
|-- .zshenv       zsh: EVERY invocation (put PATH/env here)
|-- .zprofile     zsh: login shells only
|-- .zshrc        zsh: interactive (aliases, hooks, prompt)
|-- .zlogin       zsh: login, after .zshrc
|-- .bash_profile bash: login (usually sources ~/.bashrc)
|-- .bashrc       bash: interactive non-login (aliases/hooks)
`-- .profile      sh login fallback (portable PATH/env)
```

A word on what this chapter is *not*. Dotfiles are a rabbit hole:
frameworks like oh-my-zsh, prompt themes, plugin managers, and
dotfile-syncing tools (`chezmoi`, `stow`) all exist and all have
enthusiasts. None of them is needed to make config that survives,
and each adds a layer that can itself fail on a bare server. Keep
your dotfiles small, plain, and guarded, and they travel.

## Works in terminal, fails over SSH (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

The sharpest version of the whole problem is the seam Chapter 14
flagged: a non-interactive `ssh host 'cmd'` runs a shell that
reads none of your interactive startup files, so it starts from
the server's bare `PATH`, and a tool you installed in
`~/.local/bin` is simply not found. Run against the real server,
the contrast is exact. The non-interactive command gets a PATH
without `~/.local/bin`, so `uv` is missing; forcing a login shell
with `bash -lc` reads the profile and PATH is restored:

```bash
$ ssh [hostname] 'echo $PATH'
/home/[account]/.cargo/bin:/usr/local/sbin:[...]

$ ssh [hostname] 'command -v uv || echo "uv: not found"'
uv: not found

$ ssh [hostname] 'bash -lc "echo \$PATH"'
/home/[account]/.local/bin:/home/[account]/.cargo/bin:[...]
```

Adding `bash -lc` to the last form and asking again finds it:
`command -v uv` then prints `/home/[account]/.local/bin/uv`,
because the login shell read the profile that puts `~/.local/bin`
on PATH. This is also the place to retire a piece of folklore.
"SSH reads your `.bashrc`" is only sometimes true, and not
something to rely on. The bash manual says bash *can* read
`~/.bashrc` when it detects it was started by a remote daemon
such as `sshd`, but that is a build-time option (not guaranteed
to be on), it never applies when bash runs as `sh`, and, decisive
here, a stock `~/.bashrc` on Debian and Ubuntu opens with a guard
(`case $- in *i*) ;; *) return;; esac`) that returns immediately
when the shell is non-interactive. So even where bash does source
it, the file does nothing. The net effect on a typical Ubuntu
server is the capture above: your interactive PATH edits do not
reach `ssh host 'cmd'`. Do not reason from the folklore; run the
one-line probe and read what the server actually does.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
A `PATH` entry, an alias, or a tool hook that lives only in your
interactive rc is invisible to a script, to an `ssh host 'cmd'`,
and to a cron job, because none of those reads that file. So "it
works in my terminal" is not evidence it works anywhere else, and
the failure is silent: not an error, just a command that is not
found or an option that is not set, in exactly the automated
context you were not watching. The moment a command has to run
somewhere non-interactive, assume your rc did not run.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
When something works at your prompt but fails in a script, over
SSH, or under cron, debug the seam directly instead of guessing.
Ask the failing context what it actually sees:
`ssh host 'echo $PATH'` and `bash -lc 'echo $PATH'` show the two
PATHs side by side; echoing a marker from each rc (as this
chapter's demos do) shows which files fired; and comparing an
interactive run to `bash -c '...'` locally reproduces the gap
without a server. Then fix it at the source: move the setting to
the file the failing context reads (`~/.zshenv` for env every
shell needs), or, better for anything a pipeline depends on, have
the script or Makefile set its own environment the Chapter 11
way rather than trusting a dotfile ran.
:::

## Scheduled and non-interactive contexts: cron, launchd, CI (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Scheduled jobs are the same seam, one step further from your
terminal, and the reason they surprise people is that they start
from an even barer environment than an SSH command. `cron` sets
up only a minimal environment for a job: per `crontab(5)` [-@crontabman], it
exports `SHELL=/bin/sh` and takes `HOME` and `LOGNAME` from your
`/etc/passwd` line, and it reads none of your shell startup
files. Whatever `PATH` a job gets is a minimal default from the
daemon and its `/bin/sh`, never your login `PATH`, so a job that
calls `uv` or an `~/.local/bin` tool by bare name fails with
"command not found" even though the same command works when you
type it. You can see the stripped environment a scheduled job
starts from by clearing the environment with `env -i`, which is
close to what `cron` hands a job, and contrasting it with the
non-interactive shell that already skipped your rc:

```bash
$ bash -c 'echo "$PATH"'      # non-interactive: rc skipped
/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

$ env -i bash -c 'echo "$PATH"'   # stripped env, like cron
/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
```

The fix is to set the environment where the job reads it, not in
your dotfiles. A crontab takes `name = value` lines directly, so
you set `PATH` at the top of the crontab itself (shown here as a
line you would write, not run):

```text
# crontab: set the environment the job needs, explicitly
PATH=/home/you/.local/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
0 6 * * *  cd ~/project && make report
```

The five fields before the command are cron's schedule, in
order minute, hour, day of month, month, and day of week, where
a `*` means every. So `0 6 * * *` reads as minute 0 of hour 6
on every day of every month and every weekday: 6:00 each
morning. (For a job that should run once rather than on a
repeating schedule, `at` takes a single time instead.)

macOS uses `launchd` rather than `cron` for scheduled and
GUI-launched work, and it behaves the same way for our purposes:
a process `launchd` starts inherits `launchd`'s environment, not
your shell's. On the author's Mac `launchctl getenv PATH` prints
nothing, so a Mac app or LaunchAgent begins with no PATH from
your dotfiles at all; a `launchd` job sets its environment in the
job's `plist` (an `EnvironmentVariables` key), not in `~/.zshrc`.
A CI runner is the same story a third time: it executes each step
in a non-interactive shell whose environment comes from the CI
configuration, not your dotfiles, which is exactly why CI files
spell out `PATH` and tool setup explicitly. Three different
schedulers, one rule: they do not read your interactive config,
so you give them their environment where they look for it.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
A reproducible pipeline must not depend on interactive shell
config, because the machine that reruns it, a cron job, a CI
runner, a colleague's `ssh host 'make'`, is precisely the
non-interactive shell that never reads your rc. This is the
reason Chapter 11's scripts pin their own `LC_ALL` and `TZ` and
do not assume a PATH: the script carries the environment it needs
so it behaves the same whether you launch it or a scheduler does.
Config that survives is config the job sets for itself, not
config it inherits from the human who happened to write it.
:::

## Config that survives all three

The whole chapter reduces to one rule: put each setting where the
situation that needs it will actually read it. Sort your config
by who needs it. Environment that *every* shell needs, chiefly
`PATH`, goes in the widest-read file. In zsh that is `~/.zshenv`,
read on every invocation including non-interactive scripts. Bash
has no such file: a non-interactive bash reads no user startup
file at all (only `$BASH_ENV`, if set), so the bash convention is
to put `PATH` in `~/.profile` or `~/.bash_profile` for login
shells and source `~/.bashrc` for interactive ones, and to let
child shells inherit the exported `PATH` from a login parent;
a non-interactive bash that has no such parent gets nothing from
a dotfile and must have `PATH` set for it. Interactive comforts,
aliases, prompt, key bindings, and the guarded kit hooks, go in
`~/.zshrc` or `~/.bashrc`, where they help you and stay out of
scripts. And anything a *script, job, or pipeline* needs is set
inside that script, crontab, or plist, never assumed from your
dotfiles, because the automated context does not read them.

Read the chapter's own captures back through that rule and they
line up: PATH edits reached the interactive shell but not
`bash -c` or `ssh host 'cmd'`; `.zshenv` reached even the script;
`cron` and `launchd` reached none of it. The dotfile is a
convenience for you at the prompt, the same posture Chapter 16
urged for the modern kit; the pipeline carries its own
environment, the posture Chapter 11 built in. Keep those two
straight and your config works the same whether you type the
command or a machine does.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These use the asset-pricing project from Chapter 6 and the remote
server from Chapter 14.

1. Map your own shell. In a scratch directory, put a distinct
   `echo` marker in a demo `.bash_profile`, `.bashrc`, and
   `.profile` (or the zsh four), point `HOME` (and `ZDOTDIR` for
   zsh) at it, then run `bash -lc true`, `bash -ic true`, and
   `bash -c true` and note which markers fire. Confirm the result
   against the table in the first section, and check your real
   terminal tab with `[[ -o login ]]`.

2. Break it on purpose. Add `export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"`
   to your interactive rc and confirm a tool there runs at the
   prompt. Then run the same command through `ssh localhost '...'`
   or `bash -c '...'` and watch it fail with "command not found",
   because the non-interactive shell never read the rc.

3. Fix the placement. Move that `PATH` line to the file the
   non-interactive shell reads (`~/.zshenv` for zsh), or set
   `PATH` inside the script itself the Chapter 11 way, and rerun
   the non-interactive command. Confirm it now works, and say in
   one sentence why.

4. Guard an alias. Add
   `command -v eza >/dev/null && alias ls='eza'` to your rc and
   confirm `ls` is `eza` where `eza` exists. Then open a shell on
   the bare server from Chapter 14 with the same line and confirm
   `ls` is the real `ls` with no error, the graceful degradation
   that keeps a dotfile portable.

5. Audit the pipeline. In the project, confirm the `Makefile` and
   scripts set their own environment (shebang, `LC_ALL`/`TZ`) and
   rely on nothing from your dotfiles. Explain why that is exactly
   what lets `make` reproduce the study under cron or CI, where
   your rc never runs.
